title: Antimullerian Hormone and Ovarian Reserve Testing

items.title: Why infertility evaluation begins with the woman
items.text: Women with normal fecundity usually conceive even with subnormal male reproductive function; restoring ovulation in anovulatory young women often enables conception for the couple.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Young women who are ovulating will generally conceive with a subfertile male—restoration of ovulation in young women who are infertile will often result in conception for a couple."

items.title: How to time infertility evaluation by age
items.text: Begin evaluation after 1 year of active attempts with regular cycles (12 ovulatory, exposed cycles); women older than 35 years should be evaluated after 6 months.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management Initial Evaluation of the Infertile Couple: "Women older than 35 years should be evaluated for infertility after 6 months of unsuccessful active attempts."

items.title: Why AMH is not a fertility marker
items.text: Two large studies found no correlation between AMH and fecundability; AMH predicts ovarian stimulation response (quantity) but not pregnancy (quality).
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "However, 2 large studies have not shown a correlation between AMH and fecundability. Thus, it is clear that AMH is not a "fertility marker.""

items.title: How oral contraceptives affect AMH interpretation
items.text: AMH may be lowered by up to 50% with OCP use; continuous OCP use can suppress antral follicle growth—stop pills and repeat testing in 2–3 months before major decisions.
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "AMH is lowered with the use of oral contraceptive pills, by as much as 50%, and thus may not be reflective of true ovarian reserve."

items.title: Menstrual clues to declining ovarian reserve
items.text: Regular predictable cycles usually indicate ovulation (>98%), but shortening cycle interval or new precycle spotting may signal declining reserve or luteal-phase progesterone deficiency.
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "Important questions include changes in cycle interval (a shortening of the intermenstrual interval may suggest a decline in ovarian reserve)."

items.title: How to treat anovulation in PCOS
items.text: Weight reduction is first-line; if ovulation fails or the woman is older, letrozole is the most effective first-line ovulation-induction agent per randomized trials (~85% conceive with medical therapy).
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) are best treated with weight reduction; if this fails to induce ovulation and/or the women is older, ovulation induction with letrozole is indicated."

items.title: Why pulsatile GnRH is ideal for functional hypothalamic amenorrhea
items.text: After treating thyroid disease or hyperprolactinemia, pulsatile GnRH most closely restores physiology with lower multiple-pregnancy risk than exogenous gonadotropins (though unavailable in the US).
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "For women with functional hypothalamic amenorrhea, ovulation induction with pulsatile GnRH is the most effective and physiological."

items.title: Why low AMH in young ovulatory women does not imply infertility
items.text: Decreased ovarian reserve in young women who are ovulating is not implicated in infertility or lowered fecundity; management differs from older women with diminished reserve.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Decreased ovarian reserve in young women is not implicated in infertility and/or lowered fecundity."

items.title: How to manage older women with diminished ovarian reserve
items.text: They have a shortened timeline and often need assisted reproductive technology after a brief course of medical therapy and/or intrauterine insemination.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Older women, especially those with diminished ovarian reserve, may have a shortened timeline for conception and should be managed more aggressively."

items.title: When to evaluate the male partner in infertility
items.text: Both partners should be evaluated; healthy young men are >90% likely to have sperm, so focus on treating female anovulation when PCOS is obvious—but men with risk factors or hypogonadal signs need semen analysis.
items.reference: Case 3: "Healthy young men are more than 90% likely to have a sperm in the ejaculate. Thus, the focus should be treatment of her chronic anovulation and not assessment of the man."

items.title: How to manage unexplained infertility with low AMH at age 32
items.text: Low AMH may shorten the reproductive window but does not reduce current conception chance; initial treatment is clomiphene citrate with intrauterine insemination rather than immediate IVF or gonadotropins.
items.reference: Case 6: "Given her young age, she should be treated as having "unexplained infertility." The initial treatment of unexplained infertility is ovarian stimulation with clomiphene citrate and intrauterine insemination (Answer B)."

items.title: Distinguishing hypogonadotropic hypogonadism from diminished ovarian reserve
items.text: Low AMH with low estradiol and low FSH suggests central amenorrhea; low AMH with elevated FSH and intact pituitary feedback indicates diminished ovarian reserve (not POI if still cycling).
items.reference: Case 5: "This patient clearly has diminished ovarian reserve with low AMH and elevated (although not menopausal) FSH."

items.question: A 28-year-old woman with Hashimoto disease on continuous OCPs asks about future fertility. Best course?
options: Stop OCPs and attempt conception now
Reassure that young age guarantees future fertility
items.explanation: Her autoimmune history and concern warrant ovarian reserve testing; conception should not be suggested until she is ready, and age alone is insufficient reassurance.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: B) Measure serum AMH"

items.question: AMH is 0.5 ng/mL on continuous OCPs. Best next step?
options: Stop OCPs and repeat testing in 2–3 months
items.explanation: OCPs suppress AMH; stop and repeat before counseling on menopause risk or conception prognosis—AMH does not predict spontaneous fecundability.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "Answer: A) Suggest she stop oral contraceptive pills and repeat the testing"

items.question: A young woman has AMH 0.5 ng/mL and AFC 5; male partner has low sperm count. Best course?
options: Advise them to keep trying without intervention
Controlled ovarian stimulation (e.g., clomiphene) with IUI
items.explanation: At young age, controlled ovarian stimulation with IUI can shorten time to pregnancy; AMH does not mandate IVF and egg freezing may not be necessary.
items.reference: Case 2 (Continued): "Answer: B) Recommend controlled ovarian stimulation"

items.question: A 32-year-old woman with oligomenorrhea, hirsutism, and 9 months infertility. Best management?
options: Increase intercourse to 4 times weekly only
items.explanation: Chronic oligoanovulation with PCOS features warrants ovulation induction with letrozole rather than further delay or male testing as first priority.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: D) Start a trial of letrozole for the woman"

items.question: A thin former athlete has amenorrhea 5 months after stopping OCPs: FSH 1.5, estradiol 10, AMH 0.01. Diagnosis?
items.explanation: Low gonadotropins with low estradiol indicate central cause; 5 months off OCPs is too long for simple postpill suppression; PCOS patients are not hypoestrogenic.
items.reference: Case 4: "Answer: C) Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism"

items.question: Best fertility treatment for functional hypogonadotropic hypogonadism?
items.explanation: Pulsatile GnRH replaces the missing hypothalamic signal physiologically; aromatase inhibitors/clomiphene fail when estradiol is already very low.
items.reference: Case 4 (Continued): "Answer: A) Pulsatile GnRH therapy"

items.question: A 33-year-old with irregular cycles, FSH 15, estradiol 10, AMH 0.01. Most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: Elevated FSH with low estradiol and low AMH while still cycling indicates diminished reserve, not central hypogonadism or PCOS.
items.reference: Case 5: "Answer: D) Diminished ovarian reserve"

items.question: Best next step for severely diminished ovarian reserve in a same-sex couple?
options: Evaluate partner's ovarian reserve for shared conception
items.explanation: Partner with higher reserve as gamete donor and interested partner as gestational carrier offers highest success; IVF does not fix limited oocyte pool.
items.reference: Case 5 (Continued): "Answer: B) Evaluate her partner's ovarian reserve"

items.question: 32-year-old with regular cycles, AMH 0.9, patent tubes, normal semen, 1 year infertility. Best management?
options: Keep trying with more intercourse only
items.explanation: Unexplained infertility with low but not prohibitive AMH: clomiphene plus IUI is appropriate first intervention at her age.
items.reference: Case 6: "Answer: B) Start clomiphene citrate and proceed with intrauterine insemination"

items.question: AMH primarily predicts:
options: Natural fecundability in the general population
Ovarian response to stimulation, not pregnancy per se
items.explanation: AMH reflects follicle quantity and stimulation response; large studies show no fecundability correlation.
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "AMH is a predictor of response to stimulation (in vitro fertilization cycle), but it does not correlate with pregnancy."

items.question: In the United States, infertility is typically defined as failure to conceive after:
items.explanation: Standard definition is 12 months, with earlier evaluation after 6 months if age >35.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "In the United States, infertility is typically defined as the inability to conceive after 1 year, at which time an evaluation should begin."

items.question: In infertile couples, male factor contributes:
options: In ~35% with both partners affected plus 10%–20% male-only
Only after female workup is complete
items.explanation: Male and combined factors are common; both partners should be evaluated.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "about 35% of couples have both a male and female etiology of their infertility with another 10% to 20% having a sole male factor as the identifiable cause."

items.question: First-line ovulation induction for PCOS per RCT evidence is:
items.explanation: Randomized trials identify letrozole as most effective first-line for PCOS ovulation induction.
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "Randomized controlled trials have identified letrozole as most effective first-line treatment."

items.statement: AMH concentration correlates with natural fecundability in large prospective studies.
items.explanation: Two large studies found no AMH–fecundability correlation.
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "2 large studies have not shown a correlation between AMH and fecundability."

items.statement: Oral contraceptive pills can lower AMH by as much as 50%.
items.explanation: OCP suppression of antral follicles can markedly reduce measured AMH.
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "AMH is lowered with the use of oral contraceptive pills, by as much as 50%"

items.statement: Regular, cyclic, predictable menses are associated with ovulation in greater than 98% of cases.
items.explanation: Predictable cycles strongly predict ovulation though cycle interval changes still warrant attention.
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "A history of regular, cyclic, predictable periods is associated with ovulation in greater than 98% of cases."

items.statement: Low AMH in a young ovulating woman automatically indicates current infertility.
items.explanation: Decreased reserve in young ovulatory women is not implicated in lowered fecundity.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Decreased ovarian reserve in young women is not implicated in infertility and/or lowered fecundity."

items.statement: About 85% of women with PCOS conceive with ovulation-induction drugs.
items.explanation: Medical ovulation induction is highly effective in PCOS.
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "About 85% of women with PCOS conceive with these drugs."

items.statement: Exogenous gonadotropins carry higher multiple-pregnancy and OHSS risk than pulsatile GnRH in hypothalamic amenorrhea.
items.explanation: Direct ovarian stimulation bypasses physiological feedback.
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "Gonadotropin therapy is effective, but it is much more likely than GnRH therapy to be complicated by a high risk of multiple pregnancy and ovarian hyperstimulation"

items.statement: Women older than 35 should begin infertility evaluation after 6 months of unsuccessful attempts.
items.explanation: Earlier evaluation is recommended due to shortened reproductive window.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management Initial Evaluation of the Infertile Couple: "Women older than 35 years should be evaluated for infertility after 6 months of unsuccessful active attempts."

items.statement: Amenorrhea 5 months after stopping OCPs with very low FSH and estradiol is typical postpill amenorrhea alone.
items.explanation: Prolonged suppression with hypogonadotropic pattern suggests central hypogonadism, not simple postpill recovery delay.
items.reference: Case 4: "this suppression would not persist for 5 months after stopping the pills and to this extent."

items.assertion: Assertion: AMH is a marker of ovarian follicle quantity.
items.reason: Reason: AMH directly measures oocyte genetic quality and pregnancy rate.
items.explanation: Assertion true; AMH reflects pool size not quality—reason false.
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "This suggests AMH is a marker of "quantity" but not "quality" of the oocytes."

items.assertion: Assertion: Infertility evaluation should begin with the woman.
items.reason: Reason: Male factor never contributes to couple infertility.
items.explanation: Assertion true per primacy of female evaluation; male factor is common—reason false.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Primacy of the evaluation and management of the woman."

items.assertion: Assertion: Letrozole is first-line ovulation induction for PCOS when weight loss fails or patient is older.
items.reason: Reason: Letrozole permanently destroys ovarian follicles.
items.explanation: Assertion true; letrozole briefly blocks estrogen feedback—reason false.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "ovulation induction with letrozole is indicated."

items.assertion: Assertion: AMH measured on continuous OCPs may underestimate true ovarian reserve.
items.reason: Reason: OCPs increase AMH by stimulating antral follicle growth.
items.explanation: Assertion true; OCPs lower AMH—reason false.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "Oral contraceptive pills, especially when given in a continuous fashion, can suppress antral follicle growth and AMH levels."

items.assertion: Assertion: Low FSH with low estradiol suggests hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.
items.reason: Reason: PCOS always presents with low estradiol and low AMH.
items.explanation: Assertion true; PCOS patients are hypoestrogenic only in rare contexts—reason false.
items.reference: Case 4: "the FSH level is also low in the face of a low estradiol. This combination of findings suggests a central cause of amenorrhea"

items.assertion: Assertion: Low AMH at age 32 does not eliminate current conception chances in unexplained infertility.
items.reason: Reason: Low AMH always requires immediate IVF.
items.explanation: Assertion true per Case 6; IVF is not mandatory—reason false.
items.reference: Case 6: "her chances for pregnancy at this time are not impacted by the low reserve."

items.assertion: Assertion: Pulsatile GnRH is the most physiological treatment for functional hypothalamic amenorrhea.
items.reason: Reason: Pulsatile GnRH is widely available in the United States.
items.explanation: Assertion true; agent is under FDA review and not available in US—reason false.
items.reference: Approach to the Subfertile/Infertile Woman: "This agent is not available in the United States, but it is under FDA review."

items.assertion: Assertion: Older women with diminished ovarian reserve need more aggressive management.
items.reason: Reason: AMH in older women reliably predicts they cannot conceive without donor oocytes.
items.explanation: Assertion true; AMH does not individually preclude conception—reason overstates.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Older women, especially those with diminished ovarian reserve, may have a shortened timeline for conception and should be managed more aggressively."
